pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that price objections from non-ideal customers should be ignored because the price is functioning correctly as an avatar filter. Price objections from ideal-fit customers warrant adding a lower tier. Conflating the two signals leads to incorrectly lowering prices or adding unnecessary tiers.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi frames the practitioner transition decision as a short-term versus permanent tradeoff: transitioning clients causes short-term revenue loss, but failing to transition means the founder is permanently the delivery bottleneck. The asymmetry favors transitioning.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that the sunk-cost fallacy leads founders to continue funding a bad capital allocation decision (such as a partially built SaaS product) rather than stopping; he frames stopping bad spending as potentially the best capital decision a founder can make.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that switching an entire business model is the worst-case option and should be treated as a last resort (the Armageddon button), because there are almost always intermediate fixes—such as finding better-covered customers or improving the sales close process—that address the symptom without destroying the existing revenue engine.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that serially jumping from opportunity to opportunity at the first sign of difficulty is the primary reason entrepreneurs fail to achieve large outcomes: each jump restarts the cycle at uninformed optimism, meaning the entrepreneur never passes through informed pessimism and the valley of despair to reach the upside on any single path.
